---
title: "Regression Models"
subtitle: "STA3100 Lecture 33"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: lualatex
    pdf-standard: ua-2
    geometry:
      - margin = 1in
      - paperwidth = 11in
      - paperheight = 6.875in
    header-includes:
      - \usepackage{booktabs}
      - \usepackage{sectsty}
      - \allsectionsfont{\centering}
      - \pagenumbering{gobble}
lang: en
execute:
  error: true
---


# Linear Regression in R
**Sale Prices of Single Family Houses in Gainesville**
```{r}
#| message: false

library(tidyverse)
```

```{r}
houses <- readRDS("houseSalesGNV2020.rds") 
names(houses)
houses <- as_tibble(houses)
head(houses, 12)
```

# Capturing the directional part of the street address

* Some have "`Nw`" or "`Sw`".
* Some houses have only N or S or E or W or no direction at all.
* I googled a regexp to extract directions from a street address and modified it slightly. It's pretty complicated and does more than we need, but could be useful in the future.
* Someday I'll use `regexp(..., comments = TRUE)` to explain this regexp.

```{r}
direction_regex <- ".*?(\\b(?i)((?:north|south)(?:\\h*(?:east|west))?\\b|\\b(?:east|west|[NS][EW]?|[EW]))\\b).*"
houses |> 
  mutate(direction = str_replace(street, direction_regex, "\\1")) |>
  pull(direction) |> unique()
houses <- houses |>
  mutate(direction = ifelse(str_detect(street, direction_regex), street, NA),
         direction = str_replace(direction, direction_regex, "\\1"), 
         direction = str_to_upper(direction))
houses |> group_by(direction) |> count()
```

* Now using the direction to add a factor variable for quadrant.
* If direction is not one of NE, NW, SE, or SW, then I set quadrant to `NA`.

```{r}
houses <- houses |> 
  mutate(quadrant = ifelse(direction %in% c("NE", "NW", "SE", "SW"),
                           direction, NA),
         quadrant = factor(quadrant, levels = c("NE", "NW", "SE", "SW")))
houses |> group_by(quadrant) |> count()
```

# An Egregious Outlier
* There are some pretty crazy outliers in this dataset.
```{r}
#| fig-cap: "Gainesville house prices by square footage."

opar <- par(mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 1.1, 1.1))
with(houses, plot(sqft, price))
with(houses, text(sqft, price, ifelse(price > 2e6, street, ""), pos = 4)) 
par(opar)
```

```{r}
#| fig-cap: "Gainesville house prices by square footage."

ggplot(houses, aes(x = sqft, y = price)) + 
  geom_point() +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(price > 2e6, street, "")), hjust = "inward")
```

![A house with incorrect sales data on realtor.com.](WrongPrice.png)

# Extreme Prices and Missing Predictors

* Some of the prices seem extremely high or low given the size of the house.

```{r}
houses <- houses |> 
  mutate(dpsf = price / sqft,
         dpsf_hi_lo = cut(dpsf, breaks = c(0, 50, 100, 300, 400, Inf),
                          labels = c("very_low", "low", "med", "high", "very_high")))
houses |> group_by(dpsf_hi_lo) |> summarize(n = n())

houses |> filter(dpsf < 400) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = dpsf)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5)    # Intentionally undersmoothing to see gaps.

houses |> filter(dpsf < 400) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = dpsf)) +
  geom_density(adjust = 0.8)      # Undersmoothing a bit.
```


```{r}
sqft_na <- is.na(houses$sqft)
beds_na <- is.na(houses$beds) ; baths_na <- is.na(houses$baths)
quadrant_na <- is.na(houses$quadrant)
too_low <- (houses$dpsf < 50) ; too_high <- (houses$dpsf > 300)
leave_out <- sqft_na | beds_na | baths_na | quadrant_na | too_low | too_high
```

There are

* 19 with square footage missing
* 21 with number of bedrooms missing
* 23 with number of bathrooms missing
* 15 for which I was unable to determine a quadrant
* 28 houses with price under $50 per sq ft
* 16 with price over $300 per sq ft

Altogether, these make up 80 of the 1080 observations.

* I'm going to remove these 80 observations from consideration.
  - In real life you might handle this differently.
  - Remember, outliers are often the most interesting observations.
* We will just work with the last 200 or so house sales that passed our filter.  
```{r}
myhouses <- houses |> 
  filter(!leave_out) |> 
  slice_max(date, n = 200)
```

# Two-Sample Student's t Test

Is there a difference in average price between houses in East vs West Gainesville?

```{r}
myhouses <- myhouses |> 
  mutate(EW = fct_collapse(quadrant, E = c("NE","SE"), W = c("NW", "SW")))
myhouses |> group_by(EW) |> summarize(n = n(), mean_price = mean(price))
with(myhouses, t.test(price[EW == "W"], price[EW == "E"]))
```

* Or use the formula method for `t.test()`

```{r}
t.test(price ~ EW, data = myhouses)
```

**Wait, what is Welch's two-sample t-test!?**  

* Isn't it "Student's" (Samuel Gosset's) t-test?
* Look at the help for `t.test`. This is probably not the t-test you know.
* Why might the following variances be relevant?

```{r}
with(myhouses, tapply(price, EW, var))
with(myhouses, tapply(price, EW, sd))
```

* The standard t-test and t-confidence interval (shown below) may not be appropriate here.

```{r}
t.test(price ~ EW, data = myhouses, var.equal = TRUE)
```

# Simple Linear Regression

Notice that there is a "formula" method for `plot()` and for many other functions in R.

```{r}
#| fig-cap: "Area and selling price for 208 Gainesville houses."

opar <- par(mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 0.1, 0.5)) 
plot(price ~ sqft, data = myhouses) 
par(opar)
```

A _simple_ linear regression model states that the mean of the response $Y$ is a linear (or more precisely an _affine_) function of a _single numeric_ predictor $x$.

Thus, with $\mu=E(Y|x)$, a simple linear model states that
$$ \mu=\beta_0+\beta_1 x $$

* the _coefficients_ $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ are unknown parameters.
* $\beta_1$ in interpretable as the change in $\mu$ corresponding to a 1-unit increase in $x$.
* Usually assume that $\sigma^2=\text{Var}(Y|x)$ (also unknown) is constant and does not depend on $x$.
* The data $(x_1, y_1), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$ are $n$ _independent_ pairs sampled from a population satisfying this model.
* "Fitting" a model to a dataset means using the data to estimate the unknown parameters in the model.
* Linear models are usually fit by a _least squares_ algorithm which chooses the estimators $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$ to minimize the residual sum of squares
$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i-\hat{\mu}_i)^2, \quad\text{where } \hat{\mu}_i=\hat{\beta}_0+\hat{\beta}_1 x_i
$$
* In R, linear regression models are fitted using the `lm()` function.
* The model is specified by an R formula with a tilde ("~") separating the
response variable on the left from the predictor on the right.

```{r}
lm_area <- lm(price ~ sqft, data = myhouses)
names(lm_area)
```

The least squares estimates $\hat{\beta}_0=-66,590$ and $\hat{\beta}_1=175$ can be accessed by name or by using the extractor function `coef()`.

```{r}
lm_area$coefficients
coef(lm_area)
```

* Plotting with base-R.
```{r}
#| fig-cap: "Area and selling price for 208 Gainesville houses."

opar <- par(mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 0.1, 0.1)) 
plot(price ~ sqft, data = myhouses)
abline(lm_area) 
par(opar)
```

* Plotting with `ggplot2`.
```{r}
#| fig-cap: "Area and selling price for 208 Gainesville houses."

ggplot(myhouses, aes(x = sqft, y = price)) + 
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm)
```

```{r}
summary(lm_area) # Prints a summary of the regression fit.
```

The list returned by `summary()` can be saved for further use.

```{r}
lm_area_smry <- summary(lm_area) 
names(lm_area_smry)
lm_area_smry$coefficients
lm_area_smry$coefficients[, "Std. Error"]
```

Confidence intervals for the coefficients can be computed from the summary information, but it is easier to use the `confint()` function.

```{r}
confint(lm_area)
```

* We are 95% confident that mean house price increases by between 161 and 190 dollars for every 1-square-foot increase in house area.

# Multiple Regression

A linear regression model may have several predictors. For example, in a model with three numeric predictors, $x_1$, $x_2$, and $x_3$, we have
$$ \mu=\beta_0+\beta_1 x_1+\beta_2 x_2+\beta_3 x_3 $$

* Here $\beta_1$ represents the change in $\mu$ corresponding to a 1-unit increase in $x_1$ _controlling_ for $x_2$ and $x_3$ (i.e., _while holding_ $x_2$ and $x_3$ fixed). 
* Data are 4-tuples $(x_{1, 1}, x_{1, 2}, x_{1, 3}, y_1), \ldots, (x_{n, 1}, x_{n, 2}, x_{n, 3}, y_n)$.
* $x_{ij}$ represents the value of the $j$th predictor for the $i$th observation.
* The _terms_ in the model are added to the RHS of the model formula.

```{r}
lm_abb <- lm(price ~ sqft + beds + baths, data = myhouses)
summary(lm_abb)
```

## `update()`  
The update function provides a shorthand for fitting a new model by building on an existing model. For instance, having already run
```{r}
lm_area <- lm(price ~ sqft, data = myhouses)
```
we could have replaced
```{r}
lm_abb <- lm(price ~ sqft + beds + baths, data = myhouses)
```
by
```{r}
lm_abb <- update(lm_area, . ~ . + beds + baths)
```
* In the model formula for `update()`, the periods (".") on the LHS and RHS of the tilde character represent the terms that were already on the LHS and RHS of the model formula for `lm_area`.
* We can also take away variables.
```{r}
summary(update(lm_abb, . ~ . - beds))
summary(update(lm_abb, . ~ . - baths))
```

## Comparing Nested Models

Consider the models  

* `lm_area: price ~ sqft`
* `lm_abb: price ~ sqft + beds + baths`

Model `lm_abb` says that
$$ E(\text{price})=\beta_0+\beta_1\cdot\text{sqft}+\beta_2\cdot\text{beds}+\beta_3\cdot\text{baths} $$

* Model `lm_area` is a special case of model `lm_abb` where the coefficients of `beds` ($\beta_2$) and `baths` ($\beta_3$) are both zero.
* We say that `lm_area` is _nested in_ `lm_abb`; or equiavlently, `lm_abb` _contains_ `lm_area`.
* Q : Can the _full model_ `lm_abb` be reduced to the simpler model `lm_area`?
* i.e., is there strong evidence that `beds` and `baths` help to predict `price` once we have accounted for `sqft`?

More formally, we assume
$$ E(\text{price})=\beta_0+\beta_1\cdot\text{sqft}+\beta_2\cdot\text{beds}+\beta_3\cdot\text{baths} $$
and we wish to test
$$ H_0: \beta_2=\beta_3=0\quad\text{ vs }\quad H_1: \text{either } \beta_2\neq0\text{ or }\beta_3\neq0\text{ (or both)} $$
```{r}
anova(lm_area, lm_abb, test = "F")
```

* The p-value 0.1079 indicates very little evidence against the null hypothesis.

# Factors and Dummy Variables

What if we wanted to include the quadrant (NE, NW, SE, SW) as a predictor?

In a regression model, R automatically generates \{0,1\}-valued _dummy variables_ to represent the levels of a factor. By default:

* Number of dummy variables is 1 less than the number of levels of the factor.
* The level with no dummy variable is the _control_ or _reference_ level of the
factor.
* To change reference level use base-R's `relevel()` or `forcats::fct_relevel()`.
  - For more sophisticated (and confusing) approaches, see `contrasts()`.
* The coefficient of a dummy variable represents the difference in mean response for an observation at the corresponding level of the factor vs an observation at the control level, with all other variables held constant.

```{r}
table(myhouses$quadrant)
lm_aq <- lm(price ~ sqft + quadrant, data = myhouses) 
summary(lm_aq)
```

* In this example, "NE" acts as the reference level of the `quadrant` factor and there are 3 dummy variables:
  - `quadrantNW` equals `1` if `quadrant == NW` and `0` otherwise
  - `quadrantSE` equals `1` if `quadrant == SE` and `0` otherwise
  - `quadrantSW` equals `1` if `quadrant == SW` and `0` otherwise
  
| quadrant|$q_{\text{NW}}=$ quadrantNW |$q_{\text{SE}}=$ quadrantSE |$q_{\text{SW}}=$ quadrantSW |
|:-------:|:-------------------:|:-------------------:|:-------------------:|
|NE       |0                    |0                    |0                    |
|NW       |1                    |0                    |0                    |
|SE       |0                    |1                    |0                    |
|SW       |0                    |0                    |1                    |

Let $x$ represent `sqft` and let $\mu=E(\text{price})$.

Model `lm_aq: price ~ sqft + quadrant` is then  
$$
\mu=\alpha+\beta x + \gamma_{\text{NW}}\cdot q_{\text{NW}} + \gamma_{\text{SE}}\cdot q_{\text{SE}} + \gamma_{\text{SW}}\cdot q_{\text{SW}}
$$

| quadrant|$\mu$                       |
|:-------:|:--------------------------------:|
|NE       |$\alpha+\beta x$                     |
|NW       |$(\alpha+\gamma_{\text{NW}})+\beta x$ |
|SE       |$(\alpha+\gamma_{\text{SE}})+\beta x$|
|SW       |$(\alpha+\gamma_{\text{SW}})+\beta x$ |

|$\hat{\alpha}$|$\hat{\beta}$|$\hat{\gamma}_{\text{NW}}$|$\hat{\gamma}_{\text{SE}}$|$\hat{\gamma}_{\text{SW}}$|
|:----------:|:---------:|:----------------:|:----------------:|:----------------:|
|-79116      |165        |25465             |-36110            |53282             |

* Controlling for quadrant, we estimate that on average price increases by 165 dollars per square foot increase in house area.
* Controlling for area (square-footage), we estimate that
  - houses in the NW on avg cost 25465 dollars **more** than houses in the NE
  - houses in the SE on avg cost 36110 dollars **less** than houses in the NE
  - houses in the SW on avg cost 53282 dollars **more** than houses in the NE
  
Note: "Controlling for" means holding constant. So "controlling for area" means that we are comparing costs of houses with the same square-footage.

| quadrant|$\hat{\mu}$                       |
|:-------:|:--------------------------------:|
|NE       |$-79116+165x$                     |
|NW       |$(-79116+25454)+165x=-53651+165x$ |
|SE       |$(-79116-36110)+165x=-115225+165x$|
|SW       |$(-79116+53282)+165x=-25834+165x$ |

```{r}
#| fig-cap: "Area and selling price for 208 Gainesville houses."

opar <- par(mar = c(5.1, 5.1, 0.1, 0.1), mgp = c(2.5, 1, 0), las = 0)

plot(price ~ sqft, pch = as.integer(quadrant), data = myhouses)
ab <- coef(lm_aq)
abline(ab["(Intercept)"], ab["sqft"], lty = 1) 
abline(ab["(Intercept)"] + ab["quadrantNW"], ab["sqft"], lty = 2)
abline(ab["(Intercept)"] + ab["quadrantSE"], ab["sqft"], lty = 3)
abline(ab["(Intercept)"] + ab["quadrantSW"], ab["sqft"], lty = 4) 
legend("bottomright", levels(myhouses$quadrant), pch = 1:4, lty = 1:4)

par(opar)
```

* [\textcolor{blue}{A post on plotting fitted lines with `ggplot2`}](https://aosmith.rbind.io/2018/11/16/plot-fitted-lines/)

```{r}
#| fig-cap: "Area and selling price for 208 Gainesville houses."

plot3 <- myhouses |> 
  bind_cols(fitted_price = predict(lm_aq)) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = sqft, y = price, color = quadrant)) + 
  geom_point() +
  geom_line(aes(y = fitted_price), linewidth = 1)
plot3
```

```{r}
## houses_grid <- 
##   expand.grid(sqft = range(myhouses$sqft), quadrant = unique(myhouses$quadrant))
houses_grid <- 
  expand_grid(sqft = range(myhouses$sqft), quadrant = unique(myhouses$quadrant))
houses_grid$price = predict(lm_aq, newdata = houses_grid)
houses_grid
```

```{r}
#| fig-cap: "Area and selling price for 208 Gainesville houses."

plot3b <- ggplot(myhouses, aes(x = sqft, y = price, color = quadrant)) + 
  geom_point() +
  geom_line(data = houses_grid)
plot3b
```

# Interactions

* The previous model did not include any _interaction_ between the numerical predictor `sqft` and the factor `quadrant`.
* This no-interaction model
  - allows for a different intercept for the four quadrants,
  - but forces the slope (the rate of change of mean `price` as a function of `sqft`) to be the same for all four quadrants.
* I.e., the effect of house area (`sqft`) on price does not depend the quadrant in which the house is located. In all quadrants, the mean price increases by $\beta$
dollars for each 1 sq-ft increase in the area of the house. 
* This is sometimes referred to as an _analysis of covariance_ (ANCOVA) model.
* If we add an interaction, then both the intercepts _and_ the slopes can vary by quadrant, so the effect of area on price varies by quadrant.
* Interaction model is written `price ~ sqft + quadrant + sqft:quadrant` or in abbreviated form `price ~ sqft * quadrant`.
* An interaction term `x:z` in a model formula adds a column that is the product of the `x` and `z` columns.
* In our example, `quadrant` is a factor, so `sqft:quadrant` gives the product of `sqft` with each of the `quadrant` dummy variables.
* **Remember:**
  - dummy variables can only take the values 0 and 1.
  - for any observation, **at most** one of the dummy variables $q_{\text{NW}}$, $q_{\text{SE}}$ and $q_{\text{SW}}$ is nonzero (for a house in the NE they are all zero).

Compare:

* Model `price ~ sqft + quadrant`:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu&=\alpha+\beta\cdot x + \gamma_{\text{NW}}\cdot q_{\text{NW}} + \gamma_{\text{SE}}\cdot q_{\text{SE}} + \gamma_{\text{SW}}\cdot q_{\text{SW}}\\
&= (\alpha+\gamma_{\text{NW}}q_{\text{NW}} + \gamma_{\text{SE}} q_{\text{SE}} + \gamma_{\text{SW}} q_{\text{SW}})+\beta x
\end{aligned}
$$
* Model `price ~ sqft + quadrant + sqft:quadrant`:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu=&\alpha+\beta\cdot x + \gamma_{\text{NW}}\cdot q_{\text{NW}} + \gamma_{\text{SE}}\cdot q_{\text{SE}} + \gamma_{\text{SW}}\cdot q_{\text{SW}}\\
&\quad+\delta_{\text{NW}}\cdot (q_{\text{NW}}\cdot x)+\delta_{\text{SE}}\cdot (q_{\text{SE}}\cdot x)+\delta_{\text{SW}}\cdot (q_{\text{SW}}\cdot x)\\
= &(\alpha+\gamma_{\text{NW}}q_{\text{NW}} + \gamma_{\text{SE}} q_{\text{SE}} + \gamma_{\text{SW}} q_{\text{SW}})\\
&\quad+(\beta + \delta_{\text{NW}}q_{\text{NW}} + \delta_{\text{SE}} q_{\text{SE}} + \delta_{\text{SW}} q_{\text{SW}})x
\end{aligned}
$$

So model `price ~ sqft + quadrant + sqft:quadrant` gives:

| quadrant|$\mu$                                                      |
|:-------:|:---------------------------------------------------------:|
|NE       |$\alpha+\beta x$                                           |
|NW       |$(\alpha+\gamma_{\text{NW}})+(\beta+\delta_{\text{NW}}) x$ |
|SE       |$(\alpha+\gamma_{\text{SE}})+(\beta+\delta_{\text{SE}}) x$ |
|SW       |$(\alpha+\gamma_{\text{SW}})+(\beta+\delta_{\text{SW}}) x$ |

* Remember `price ~ sqft * quadrant` is shorthand for this model.

```{r}
lm_aqi <- lm(price ~ sqft * quadrant, data = myhouses) 
summary(lm_aqi)$coefficients
```

|$\hat{\alpha}$|$\hat{\beta}$|$\hat{\gamma}_{\text{NW}}$|$\hat{\gamma}_{\text{SE}}$|$\hat{\gamma}_{\text{SW}}$|$\hat{\delta}_{\text{NW}}$|$\hat{\delta}_{\text{SE}}$|$\hat{\delta}_{\text{SW}}$|
|:----------:|:---------:|:----------------:|:----------------:|:----------------:|:---------:|:---------:|:---------:|
|45376       |76         |-82156            |-1613             |-124498           |80          |-17        |113        |


| quadrant|$\hat{\mu}$                            |
|:-------:|:-------------------------------------:|
|NE       |$45376+76x$                            |
|NW       |$(45376-82156)+(76+80)x=-36780+156x$   |
|SE       |$(45376-1613)+(76-17)x=43763+59x$      |
|SW       |$(45376-124498)+(76+113)x=-79122+189x$ |

Interpretation is more complicated now. We estimate that mean house price:

* in NE GNV increases on average by \$76 per sq ft increase in area.
* in NW GNV increases on average by \$156 per sq ft increase in area. 
* in SE GNV increases on average by \$59 per sq ft increase in area.
* in SW GNV increases on average by \$189 per sq ft increase in area.

```{r}
#| fig-cap: "Area and selling price for 208 Gainesville houses."

opar <- par(mar = c(5.1, 5.1, 0.1, 0.1), mgp = c(2.5, 1, 0), las = 0)

plot(price ~ sqft, pch = as.integer(quadrant), data = myhouses)
ab <- coef(lm_aqi)
abline(ab["(Intercept)"], ab["sqft"], lty = 1)
abline(ab["(Intercept)"] + ab["quadrantNW"], ab["sqft"] + ab["sqft:quadrantNW"], lty = 2) 
abline(ab["(Intercept)"] + ab["quadrantSE"], ab["sqft"] + ab["sqft:quadrantSE"], lty = 3) 
abline(ab["(Intercept)"] + ab["quadrantSW"], ab["sqft"] + ab["sqft:quadrantSW"], lty = 4) 
legend("topleft", levels(myhouses$quadrant), pch = 1:4, lty = 1:4)

par(opar)
```

```{r}
houses_grid$price = predict(lm_aqi, newdata = houses_grid) 
houses_grid
```

```{r}
#| fig-cap: "Area and selling price for 208 Gainesville houses."

plot4 <- ggplot(myhouses, aes(x = sqft, y = price, color = quadrant)) + 
  geom_point() +
  geom_line(data = houses_grid)
plot4
```

# Model Comparisons

```{r}
anova(lm_area, lm_aq, lm_aqi)
```

\raggedright Note that   
\centering
`lm_area: price ~ sqft`    
\raggedright is nested within  
\centering `lm_aq: price ~ sqft + quadrant`  
\raggedright which is nested within  
\centering `lm_aqi: price ~ sqft * quadrant`

* Second p-value (0.005) tests interaction model `price ~ sqft * quadrant`
against additive model `price ~ sqft + quadrant`
  - test of $H_0$: $\delta_{\text{NW}}=\delta_{\text{SE}}=\delta_{\text{SW}}=0$
* First p-value (0.003) tests additive model `price ~ sqft + quadrant` against the model `price ~ sqft`
  - Assuming $\delta_{\text{NW}}=\delta_{\text{SE}}=\delta_{\text{SW}}=0$, tests $H_0$: $\gamma_{\text{NW}}=\gamma_{\text{SE}}=\gamma_{\text{SW}}=0$.
